checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the content of the 15 slides. Each section title and the associated text content (lists, tables, headings) can be directly traced back to the source PDF. There are no fabricated sentences or paragraphs.
I verified all handles (@NCDIT, @BroadbandIO, @ncicenter) and the URL (it.nc.gov) on Slide 15 against the output. The information is identical to the source document.
The output contains 15 sections corresponding to the 15 pages in the source PDF. No content appears to have been omitted.
Tables in the output (e.g., the Benefit/Action tables on slides 4-14) match the row count and data of the source tables exactly.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text within each slide's section is complete. Bullet points, table headers, and body text have all been captured without significant omission.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content is highly accurate. Minor differences like whitespace or the normalization of hyphens (e.g., 'non-GICC' vs 'non ‐GICC') do not alter the meaning and are acceptable under the provided guidelines.

checks.a_analysis: There is significant content duplication and unnecessary injection on the first page. The NC DIT logo is represented three times: once as an image element, once as a descriptive text string ('NC DIT Logo') inside a layout table, and a third time as a transparent pixel image with descriptive alt text. Additionally, the presentation title is present in the metadata and again in the layout table. While metadata duplication is acceptable, the triple representation of the logo is a structural defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document on page 1 contains a single graphic logo for NC DIT and the title text.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output contains an image element (index 0), a table cell with the text 'NC DIT Logo' (index 1), and another image element with descriptive alt text (index 2), all for the same single logo on Page 1.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 'Title Slide', Page 1, elements index 0, 1, and 2.

checks.a_analysis: The heading hierarchy is highly inconsistent throughout the document. Sections 4 through 11 use internal Heading level 2 (H2) elements that repeat or expand upon the section title. In contrast, sections 12 through 14 omit internal heading elements entirely, promoting the full goal text to the section title. This creates a fragmented and inconsistent navigational structure for screen reader users, where some content is identified via headings and other content is not, despite having the same logical level in the source document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document uses consistent slide titles for all goals (e.g., Goal 1.1, Goal 1.2... Goal 2.2, Goal 2.3).
checks.b_evidence_output: Sections 4-11 contain H2 elements (e.g., 'heading-1' for Goal 1.1), while Sections 12-14 (Goal 2.2 - 2.4) contain no heading elements within their 'elements' list, only the 'title' field of the section object.

checks.a_analysis: The tables representing the goals and actions (Goals 1.1 through 2.4) are correctly structured with appropriate header rows and data cells. Although page 1 uses a layout table not present in the source, the meaningful data tables are accurate.

The reading order follows the logical flow of the presentation slides from title to the final contact information. The layout of the 'Let's Connect!' page is correctly linearized into a logical sequence.
The lists on the Timeline and Goals slides (Pages 2 and 3) are correctly identified and converted as unordered lists with the appropriate number of items.

The URL 'it.nc.gov' on the final slide is captured as plain text within a paragraph element rather than a functional, clickable link. This is a failure to preserve the semantic utility of a clearly visible URL.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully preserves the visual organization of the source PDF. The multi-page slide deck is correctly converted into a single-column scrolling format where each slide's content follows the original sequence. Information is logically grouped under appropriate headings, and the flow of information from goals to specific actions is maintained.

The tables throughout the document, specifically those listing Benefits and Actions for each goal (slides 4-14), are rendered correctly in the HTML. They maintain a clear two-column structure with distinct headers and row separation. For example, Goal 1.7 correctly displays two rows of data within the table structure, ensuring readability and comprehension.

Several meaningful images are visually broken in the HTML output, appearing as large green placeholder boxes. This is most prominent in the 'Let's Connect!' section where the social media icons (Twitter, YouTube, Facebook, LinkedIn) are not rendered. Additionally, the NC DIT logo, which appears consistently in the source PDF, is replaced by large green boxes in the Goal 2.2, Goal 2.3, and Let's Connect! sections of the HTML, despite rendering correctly in earlier sections.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains various social media icons on page 15 ('Let's Connect!') and a recurring NC DIT logo on the bottom right of pages 2-15.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows large green placeholder boxes instead of the social media icons in the 'Let's Connect!' section and instead of the NC DIT logo in sections Goal 2.2 and Goal 2.3.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections Goal 2.2, Goal 2.3, and Let's Connect!

checks.a_analysis: A thorough visual inspection of the HTML rendering shows no instances of duplicated body content. Each goal, table, and descriptive paragraph appears only once in its designated section. While headings appear in the Table of Contents and the body, this is an expected accessibility feature and not a duplication defect.
